title: LockBit successor indicators
dataSources: EDR, intel feeds, dark web scrapes (where permitted)
spl: | from datamodel Risk.All_Risk | search normalized_risk_object IN ("$user$", "$dest$") starthoursago=168  | stats count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(search_name) as "Search Name" values(risk_message) as "Risk Message" values(analyticstories) as "Analytic Stories" values(annotations._all) as "Annotations" values(annotations.mitre_attack.mitre_tactic) as "ATT&CK Tactics" by normalized_risk_object | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
description: This detection aligns with ESCU 2025–2026 analytic story coverage for: LockBit successor indicators. Hunting content for post-LockBit ecosystem behaviors and rebrands.
value: This detection aligns with ESCU 2025–2026 analytic story coverage for: LockBit successor indicators. Hunting content for post-LockBit ecosystem behaviors and rebrands.
implementation: ESCU Hunting detection — not designed for automated alerting. Run ad-hoc or on a low-frequency schedule from Splunk Enterprise Security for analyst-driven investigation. Requires EDR, intel feeds, dark web scrapes (where permitted) ingested and CIM-normalized.

references.title: Splunk ES: Risk scoring and data model

knownFalsePositives: Legitimate admin tools, vulnerability scanners, scheduled jobs, and patch automation can look like the same patterns as real attacks — we verify change tickets, maintenance windows, and known service accounts before we treat something as a true break-in.
detailedImplementation:
Enterprise Security Detection Rule

"LockBit successor indicators" is a Hunting detection designed for analyst-driven threat hunting rather than automated alerting, sourced from the Splunk Enterprise Security Content Update (ESCU). Hunting detections are not intended for automated alerting. They are run on-demand or on a low-frequency schedule by security analysts investigating specific threat hypotheses.

Prerequisites

• Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x or later with the ES Content Update (ESCU) app installed and up to date.
• Data sources: EDR, intel feeds, dark web scrapes (where permitted). Must be ingested into Splunk and normalized to the Common Information Model (CIM) via the appropriate Technology Add-on.
• Security domain (threat): Configure threat intelligence feeds and verify the Threat Intelligence data model is populated for IOC matching.
• MITRE ATT&CK: T1486. Review the ATT&CK matrix for adjacent techniques to identify detection coverage gaps in your environment.

Deployment

1. In Enterprise Security, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management.
2. Search for "LockBit successor indicators" or filter by Analytic Story.
3. Hunting detections are typically left disabled for automated scheduling. Instead, run them on-demand from the Search bar or schedule at low frequency (daily or weekly).
4. Review results manually — hunting detections cast a wider net and expect analyst judgment to separate signal from noise.
5. When results warrant further investigation, create a Notable Event manually or initiate your incident response workflow.

Tuning and False Positive Management

Known false positives for this detection: Legitimate admin tools, vulnerability scanners, scheduled jobs, and patch automation can look like the same patterns as real attacks — we verify change tickets, maintenance windows, and known service accounts before we treat something as a true break-in.

• Hunting detections are expected to produce broader result sets that require analyst interpretation. Focus on refining the search scope (time range, specific hosts or users) rather than eliminating all noise.
• Maintain a hunting journal documenting hypotheses tested, results found, and detection improvements identified.
• Share high-fidelity findings with the detection engineering team to convert recurring hunting patterns into automated TTP or Anomaly detections.

Analyst Response Workflow

When reviewing Hunting results:

1. Run the search manually or review scheduled results. Evaluate each returned event against your threat hypothesis — not every result indicates compromise.
2. Cross-reference findings with current threat intelligence, recent security advisories, and outputs from other detection sources.
3. If suspicious activity is confirmed, create a Notable Event or escalate directly to your incident response process with the supporting evidence.
4. Document the hunting exercise: hypothesis, data sources queried, time range, findings, and any detection engineering improvements identified.

About the SPL Query Shown Above

The SPL displayed for this use case is the Risk Investigation drilldown search — it queries the Risk data model to show all risk events associated with a specific entity. This is the search analysts use during investigation to understand what contributed to an entity’s risk score.

The actual detection logic is packaged within the ESCU Correlation Search definition and runs automatically on schedule. To view or modify the detection’s underlying search logic, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management and click on the detection name.

Validation

Compare a sample of **risk** events in Enterprise Security to the same entity’s line in the **Risk Analysis** view. For notables, cross-check the **Incident Review** queue. Confirm underlying endpoint or network data is present using:

```spl
| tstats count where index=* by index, sourcetype | sort -count
```

Verify the parent correlation search is running as scheduled:

```spl
index=_audit action="alert_fired" ss_name="*"
| stats count by ss_name, trigger_time | sort -trigger_time
```
grandmaExplanation: We use our security program’s detections to catch activity tied to “LockBit successor indicators” when someone tries that attacker move in our environment, so we can respond before the damage spreads.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count from datamodel=Risk.All_Risk by All_Risk.normalized_risk_object span=1d
| sort 0 - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Enterprise Security Risk data model (All_Risk) so tstats on Risk completes reliably.
